title: Shared-core pre-edit.py branches on Claude-native tool names, bypassing the normalize_tool seam it sits behind
evidence: hostapi.py establishes canonical tool categories ('EXEC|WRITE|EDIT|READ|OTHER') as the seam every shared entry should reason through, and pre-edit.py imports get_host()/normalize_tool_input — yet its guard logic branches on raw native names: `if tool == "MultiEdit"` (lines 53, 119), `if tool == "NotebookEdit"` (line 86). The escape is rationalized in a comment ('Codex does not register this entry at all'), so host-coupled string checks live inside the host-neutral core, held safe only by a registration convention in a different plugin's hooks.json.
impact: Latent third-host bug and pattern drift: any future host that registers this entry (or a Codex change routing edits through it) gets guard semantics keyed to another host's tool names — MultiEdit-batch and NotebookEdit refusals silently never fire, weakening H-05/H-18 on that host with no signal. It also sets the precedent that shared core may hardcode native names when a comment promises non-registration.
recommendation: Either move the name-dependent branching behind the seam (e.g. a Host method exposing edit-op semantics, as iter_file_ops already does for writes) or add an explicit host gate at the top of pre-edit (`if get_host().name != "claude": exit 0` with a comment), making the Claude-only assumption enforced in code rather than by remote registration convention.
acceptance_criteria: pre-edit.py contains no raw native tool-name comparisons, or an in-code host gate makes its Claude-only scope self-enforcing
